coredata.dc:title: A carbon dot based biosensor for melamine detection by fluorescence resonance energy transfer
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Dai, Haichao
Shi, Yan
Wang, Yilin
Sun, Yujing
Hu, Jingting
Ni, Pengjuan
Li, Zhuang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we constructed a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system between amino-functionalized carbon dots (C-dots) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). In this system, C-dots were treated as energy donors, while AuNPs were treated as energy acceptors. We optimized some important factors including incubation time, AuNPs concentration and media pH, which would affect the efficiency of the FRET system. Under the optimized experimental conditions, melamine could be detected based on fluorescence intensity of C-dots. We could get a linear relationship between 50nM and 500nM and the detection limit was 36nM. The proposed method was applied to the determination of melamine in milk samples with satisfactory results. Compared with previous reports, the proposed method manifested great advantages including high sensitivity, short analysis time, low cost and ease of operation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Melamine
Amino-functionalized carbon dots (C-dots)
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET)
Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and spectral characterizations of AuNPs and C-dots
Fluorescence quenching effect of AuNPs on C-dots
Optimization of the experimental conditions for the detection of melamine
Analysis of melamine in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Mechanism for the detection of melamine based on FRET: (a) the fluorescence of C-dots was quenched by AuNPs through FRET; and (b) the fluorescence of C-dots was enhanced when melamine was incubated with AuNPs before C-dots was added.
TEM images of AuNPs: (A) in the absence of melamine, (B) in the presence of 500nM melamine, (C) in the presence of 2μM melmaine. (D) An AFM image of the C-dots on mica.

(A) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of the aqueous dispersion of C-dots. Inset: images of the C-dots dispersion under visible (left) and UV light (right, 365nm). (B) Normalized PL spectra at excitation wavelengths from 340nm to 400nm on the left in 10nm increment. Inset: PL emission spectra of C-dots.
Normalized fluorescence spectrum (λ exc =350nm) of C-dots (a) and adsorption spectrum of AuNPs (b).
Fluorescence spectra: (a) 0.075mgmL−1 C-dots, (b) a +2μM melamine, (c) a +1.2nM AuNPs, and (d) a +1.2nM AuNPs +2μM melamine in 0.01M HEPES buffer (pH 8.0) (λ exc =350nm).
The effect of the concentration of AuNPs on the fluorescence enhanced efficiency. C-dots: 0.075mgmL−1, AuNPs: 0, 0.8, 1.2, 1.6, 2.0, 2.4, 2.8, 3.2nM, melamine: 2μM, pH: 8.0.

(A) The effect of pH on the fluorescence C-dots in the absence and presence of 2μM melamine. (B) The effect of pH on the fluorescence enhanced efficiency. C-dots: 0.075mgmL−1, AuNPs: 1.2nM, melamine: 2μM, pH value: 6.8, 7.1, 7.4, 7.7, 8.0, 8.2.

Fluorescence spectra of the C-dots-AuNPs system in the presence of different concentrations of melamine (λ exc =350nm). From a to m: 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25, 0.3, 0.35, 0.4, 0.45, 0.5, 1, 2μM. AuNPs: 1.2nM; pH: 8.0. Inset: the enhanced fluorescence intensities as a function of the concentration of melamine.

Selectivity of the C-dots-AuNPs FRET system toward melamine. The concentration of melamine and interferences were both 400nM. (1) Ca2+, (2) vitamin C, (3) cysteine, (4) Fe2+, (5) Na+, (6) lactose, (7) Zn2+, (8) Mg2+, (9) Cl−, (10) glycine, (11) glucose, (12) histidine, (13) SO4 2−, (14) NO3 −, (15) K+, (16) Fe3+, (17) melamine.
Comparison of different methods for the determination of melamine.

row.entry: Colorimetry/UCA and TNBS tailored Au nanoparticles

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Capillary zone electrophoresis using electrospray ionization-mass spectrometry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The application of the proposed method for analysis of raw milk and milk powder spiked with different amounts of melamine.

head.ce:title: A carbon dot based biosensor for melamine detection by fluorescence resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100039, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we constructed a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system between amino-functionalized carbon dots (C-dots) and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs). In this system, C-dots were treated as energy donors, while AuNPs were treated as energy acceptors. We optimized some important factors including incubation time, AuNPs concentration and media pH, which would affect the efficiency of the FRET system. Under the optimized experimental conditions, melamine could be detected based on fluorescence intensity of C-dots. We could get a linear relationship between 50nM and 500nM and the detection limit was 36nM. The proposed method was applied to the determination of melamine in milk samples with satisfactory results. Compared with previous reports, the proposed method manifested great advantages including high sensitivity, short analysis time, low cost and ease of operation.

ce:section.ce:para: Melamine (C3H6N6) is used primarily in the synthesis of melamine formaldehyde resins for manufacturing laminates, plastics, coatings, glues or adhesives, dishware, and kitchenware. However, because of its high nitrogen contents (66mass%), it is known to be illegally added to dairy products and animal feed to artificially increase the measured protein content. Unfortunately, melamine will result in the formation of kidney insoluble crystals in kidney, ultimately causing the formation of kidney stones [1]. The ingestion of melamine at levels above the safety limit (20μM in the USA and EU; 8μM for infant formula in China) can induce renal failure and death in infants. Thus, development of a sensitive method for melamine detection and qualification is quite necessary.

Methods developed for melamine detection had been reported massively. Conventional methods for melamine analysis reported to date involved gas chromatography [2], liquid chromatography [3], liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry [4], capillary electrophoresis [5], NMR spectroscopy [6], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [7], surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy [8], colorimetry [9] and surface plasmon resonance [10]. However, because of their requirement of expensive, complicated instruments, tedious procedures for sample pretreatment or preconcentration, many of these assays are not adaptable to routine analysis.

Fluorescent method has been proved to be a powerful optical technique for the trace detection of analytes due to its high sensitivity, simple instruments, easy operation, and the ability to measure multiple fluorescence properties. Among them, a number of fluorescence-based assays rely on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) spectroscopic technique, which occurs when the emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor are overlapped to a certain extent. FRET has been widely used in various research areas, such as nucleic acid detection [11–13], immunoassay [14–16] and ion detection [17,18]. To date, there are only few reports regarding the FRET detection of melamine.

Most of these FRET-based melamine sensing systems are primarily based on the use of fluorescence from organic dyes [19,20]. However, most organic fluorophores suffer from photobleaching and poor stability in the ambient environment, which results in irreproducible fluorescence signals for analysis. To overcome these shortcomings, some types of nanomaterials, such as quantum dots (QDs) [21–23], have been used as signaling probe. Even though, this method still suffers from some drawbacks. For example, the preparation process is relatively complex and the application of quantum dots is still controversial due to the relatively high toxicity.

Carbon dots (C-dots), a newly emerging form of carbon nanomaterials have inspired intense research efforts in various fields which can be produced inexpensively by many approaches [24–27]. Compared to traditional QDs and organic dyes, photoluminescent C-dots are superior in terms of high aqueous solubility, robust chemical inertness, easy functionalization, high resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [24,28]. The applications of C-dots in fluorescent sensing of cations and anions have also been widely reported [29,30]. To the best of our knowledge, FRET employing carbon dots as the fluorescence donor has rarely been reported.

In this work, a novel “turn-on” fluorescence sensor for melamine was developed based on melamine-induced decrease of the FRET efficiency between gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) and the C-dots. Upon addition of C-dots into AuNPs solution, the C-dots are prone to get close to the surface of AuNPs, resulting in the fluorescence quenching. Melamine contains amino groups, which can compete with the C-dots, leading the restoration of the fluorescence. This method shows apparent advantages in environmental protection, operability and sensitivity. What is more, the present method is also appropriate for the determination of melamine in real samples.

Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O) was acquired from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Melamine was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.). Vitamin C, Glycine, l-histidine and l-cysteine were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. Trisodium citrate, Glucose, lactose, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, KNO3, NaOH, FeCl2, NaCl, FeCl3·6H2O, Na2SO4 and MgCl2 were obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company. Ultrapure water obtained from a Millipore water purification system (≥18MΩ, Milli-Q, Millipore) was used in all runs.

The fluorescence spectra were obtained using a PerkinElmer LS 55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer Co.). UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained with a Cary 500 UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Varian, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on an ESCALAB MKII spectrometer (VG Co., UK) with Al Kα X-ray radiation as X-ray source for excitation. FT-IR spectra were measured on a VERTEX 70 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) image was acquired by Dimension Icon (Veeco Instruments, USA) in tapping mode.

AuNPs were prepared by the citrate reduction of HAuCl4 using the method according to the literature [31]. All glassware used in the preparation of AuNPs was cleaned with freshly prepared 3:1 HCl/HNO3. Typically, 100mL aqueous solution containing 1mM HAuCl4 was firstly heated to boiling, and then 10mL of 38.8mM trisodium citrate was rapidly injected. The mixture was further refluxed for 30min and cooled to room temperature under stirring. The obtained wine-red solution was stored at 4°C in the refrigerator for further use.

Fluorescent C-dots were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of histidine. In a typical synthesis, 2.5g histidine was dissolved in 25mL NaOH (0.5molL−1) solution and then transferred into a 50-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for a period of 12h. The C-dots were collected by removing the large dots through centrifugation and then dialyzed against ultra-pure water through a dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut-off=1000) for 48h to remove the excess precursors and resulting small molecules. The resultant C-dots was maintained at 4°C for further characterization and use.

The quantum yield (QY) of the as-prepared C-dots was measured according to an established procedure [32]. Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (literature quantum yield 0.54 at 360nm) was chose as a standard. Absolute values are calculated using the standard reference sample that has a fixed and known fluorescence quantum yield value, according to the following equation:
ϕ x
=
ϕ
std
I x
A x
A
std
I
std
η x 2
η
std
2
where φ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical density. The subscript “std” refers to standard with known QY and “x” for the sample. In order to minimize re-absorption effects, absorbencies in the 10mm fluorescence cuvette were kept under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength (360nm).Procedures for the determination of melamine

A stock solution of melamine (10mM) was prepared in water. Various concentrations of melamine were obtained by serial dilution of the stock solution. For the determination of melamine, 200μL AuNPs (3.0nM), 200μL 10mM (pH 8.0) HEPES buffer solution and 50μL various amount of melamine solution were mixed in a series of 600μL centrifuge tube. After stabilizing for 30min, 50μL 0.75mgmL−1 fluorescent C-dots were introduced to the tube and further incubated for 5min. The fluorescence intensity (at 438nm) of the mixture solutions was recorded with an excitation at 350nm. The procedures for all the optimization and control experiments are the same as described above.

Milk samples were prepared according to a previous report with a minor modification [22]. Typically, 5mL of raw milk or 5.0mg of milk powder was placed into a 10mL centrifuge tube, and 1mL of 2M trichloroacetic acid was introduced. After precipitating the protein by sonication for 10min, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 10min. The supernatants were adjusted to pH 7.0 with NaOH, further filtered with 0.22μm filter and diluted 25-fold before used for the detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and spectral characterizations of AuNPs and C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1A displays the typical TEM images of the AuNPs in the absence of melamine. It can be seen that the as-prepared AuNPs are spherical in shape, monodisperse, and fairly uniform in size (about 13nm). In the presence of a low concentration of melamine (500nM), the AuNPs are still in a dispersed state (Fig. 1B). However, upon addition of a high concentration of melamine (2μM), the AuNPs come into aggregation to some extent (Fig. 1C), which indicates the interaction between melamine and AuNPs.

The typical AFM image of the as-prepared C-dots solution is displayed in Fig. 1D. The size of the C-dots is ranging from 3nm to 5nm according to the section analysis. The XPS spectrum of the nanoparticles (Fig. S1) exhibits the surface composition and elemental analysis for resultant C-dots. It shows that the synthesized C-dots are composed of elements C, O, N and Na. The element Na may come from NaOH. The three peaks at about 287.8, 403.0, and 535.3eV shown in the XPS full scan spectrum correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. Furthermore, FTIR spectra are used to identify the surface functional groups of the obtained C-dots. As shown in Fig. S2, peak at 1609, 1492, and 1384cm−1 are assigned to the amide I, CO, amide II, N–H and amide III, C–N stretching vibrations, respectively. The broad bands centered at 3401, 3231, 3130cm−1, and the peak at 1088cm−1 suggest the existence of –OH, –NH2, –NH3 + and C–O. The FTIR results mentioned above demonstrate that the surfaces of C-dots are coated with carboxylic groups and amide groups.

Fig. 2A shows the UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the C-dots. The prepared C-dots solution shows a broad absorption peak centered at 280nm (black line), which is possibly due to the relatively broad size distribution of C-dots [25]. Note that a strong PL emission spectrum centered at 438nm (red line) is observed when it is excited at 350nm (blue line), indicating that the C-dots are fluorescent. The quantum yields (QY) is determined to be 10.7% with quinine sulfate as a reference. The inset photograph in Fig. 2A shows the optical images of the aqueous dispersion of C-dots under visible light (left) and a UV lamp (irradiated by a UV-light with 365nm) (right), respectively. The bright blue PL of C-dots is strong enough to be easily distinguished with the naked eye. Fig. 2B shows the normalized PL emission spectra of the C-dots dispersion with various excitation wavelengths from 340nm to 400nm on the left in 10nm increments. It is seen that the emission spectrum of the C-dots changes from 438nm to 468nm. As the excitation wavelength increases, the emission peak position shifts to longer wavelengths. The maximum fluorescence emission intensity can be obtained when it is excited at 350nm. Then the intensity gradually decreases. The excitation wavelength dependence of the emission wavelength and intensity is a common phenomenon observed in carbon-based quantum dots [33]. Furthermore, the effect of pH on the fluorescence property of C-dots is investigated (Fig. S3A). The fluorescence intensity of the C-dots dispersion decreases upon regulating pH value from 1 to 12, and the fluorescence emission peak gradually red-shifts with increasing pH, which indicates that the fluorescence characteristics of the C-dots strongly depend on the pH value. Fig. S3B shows the response of PL intensity to continuous irradiation at a wavelength of 350nm for 30min. The fluorescence intensity almost remains unchanged compared with the initial value, which indicates that the C-dots possess good photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching effect of AuNPs on C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: In this FRET sensing system, we employed AuNPs as the fluorescence acceptor (quencher). AuNPs have been of great interest because of their high extinction coefficient [34] and a broad absorption spectrum in a visible light that is overlapped with the emission wavelength of common energy donors. AuNPs are also proved to have a superior quenching efficiency, extending the effective energy transfer distance up to 20nm [22,35,36]. Fig. 3 shows the absorption spectrum of AuNPs and fluorescence emission spectrum of the carbon dots, respectively. It can be seen that the emission spectrum of C-dots could overlap with the absorption spectrum of AuNPs to some extent, indicating the potential to produce efficient energy transfer.

Upon addition of amino-functionalized C-dots into the AuNPs solution, C-dots nanoparticles can be adsorbed onto the surface of citrate-stabilized AuNPs to form a C-dots-AuNPs assembly due to the Au-N interaction. As a result, the fluorescence of C-dots can be quenched by AuNPs through FRET in several minutes (Fig. S4). As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots-AuNPs system is proportional to the concentration of AuNPs and quenching constant (K sv) was calculated according to the Stern–Volmer equation:
F 0
F
=
K
sv
× [ Q ] + 1
where F 0 and F are the fluorescent intensity of C-dots in the absence and presence of AuNPs, respectively. [Q] is the concentration of the quencher (AuNPs). The plot of the relationship between F 0/F and the [AuNPs] was shown in Fig. S5 as an inset, and the Stern–Volmer equation was fitted as:
F 0
F
= 0.746
[ AuNPs ]
( nM ) + 1
( R = 0.9916 )
From the slope of the linear plot, the quenching constant K sv was calculated to be 0.746×109 M−1.

The effect of melamine on the FRET system has been investigated. Fig. 4 depicts the fluorescence spectra of C-dots, C-dots-melamine, C-dots-AuNPs and C-dots-AuNPs-melamine under the same experimental conditions. As shown in this figure, when excited at 350nm, C-dots show strong fluorescence at 438nm (curve a). As expected, once AuNPs is introduced, the fluorescence of C-dots is significantly decreased due to the FRET process between C-dots and AuNPs (curve c). Upon addition of melamine, melamine molecules can interact with AuNPs via covalent binding of the amino group (NH2–) of melamine to the surface of AuNPs, which prevents the interaction of C-dots and AuNPs, thus reduces the FRET effect, resulting in the increase of the fluorescence intensity (curve d). To get rid of the possible adverse impact on the fluorescence intensity caused by melamine, melamine is also employed to the C-dots solution as a contrast. The intense fluorescence indicates that melamine causes negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots (curve b). The evolution of fluorescence intensity of the four solutions mentioned above demonstrates the feasibility of the strategy. Therefore, we speculate that a turn-on fluorescence method can be proposed for the determination of melamine. The mechanism for the detection of melamine based on FRET was shown in Scheme 1 . Upon addition of C-dots into AuNPs, the fluorescence of C-dots was quenched due to the FRET between the C-dots and AuNPs (Scheme 1A). When melamine was introduced to AuNPs before C-dots were added, less C-dots could be adsorbed on the surface of AuNPs and resulted in the fluorescence enhancement (Scheme 1B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the experimental conditions for the detection of melamine
ce:section.ce:para: The parameters including incubation time, AuNPs concentration and media pH are optimized for the C-dots-AuNPs system. At room temperature, the time dependent fluorescence of the reaction between C-dots and AuNPs is investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, the fluorescence of C-dots is obviously quenched by AuNPs in 1min and almost remained unchanged in the next 9min. To ensure the consistency throughout the experiment and obtain stable signal, 5min is chosen as the optimum incubation time.

Besides, the effect of the concentration of AuNPs on the fluorescence enhancement is also systemically investigated (Fig. 5 ). It is obvious that the fluorescence enhanced efficiency, which is defined as (F − F 0)/F 0, and F and F 0 represent the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of melamine, gets stronger gradually with increasing the concentration of AuNPs until the concentration reached 1.2nM. When the concentration of AuNPs exceeds 1.2nM, the fluorescence enhanced efficiency changes in the opposite trend. This is because the presence of excess amount of AuNPs would not be protected by melamine molecules sufficiently. Thus, carbon dots would have more opportunities to be closer to the AuNPs, inducing fluorescence quenching more thoroughly. According to the above analysis, 1.2nM of AuNPs is chosen as the optimum AuNPs concentration.

In addition, the fluorescent intensity of C-dots is closely related to the pH value of media. As reported in the literature [37], the pH value of media affects the interaction of AuNPs with small molecules. Since melamine is a weak base with a pKa of 9.0, the pH value will greatly affect the form of melamine in aqueous solution. Melamine will be hydrolyzed together with the gradual loss of amino groups under strong acidic media or strong basic media. Finally the chemical structure will be transformed from melamine to cyanuric acid, which could not interact with AuNPs [37]. Taking this point into consideration, pH values ranging from 6.8 to 8.2 are investigated to seek the optimum experimental condition. Fig. 6A shows fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the absence and presence of melamine in different pH media. It seems that the addition of melamine only does not cause significant change to the C-dots fluorescence. However, such kind of tremendous differences will emerge when AuNPs are introduced. The fluorescence enhanced efficiency (F − F 0)/F 0 is shown in Fig. 6B. Under the slightly alkaline environment (especially when pH value is ∼8.0), the (F − F 0)/F 0 value shows much greater enhancement.

The fluorescence spectra of the mixture in the presence of different amounts of melamine under the optimum conditions are shown in Fig. 7 . As displays in this figure, with increasing the concentration of melamine, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots increases gradually. The plot of the fluorescence enhanced efficiency (F − F 0)/F 0 against the concentration of added melamine is shown as an inset in Fig. 7. It is clear that the fluorescence enhanced efficiency exhibits a linear response to the melamine concentration in the range of 50–500nM, the calibration curve can be express as (F − F 0)/F 0 =0.01113+0.000524c (c: nM). The limit detection (3σ/s) for melamine is 36nM, where σ represents the standard deviation of eight blank measurements, and s is the slope of the calibration curve. These analytical parameters are comparable or even better than those reported in the literature (shown in Table 1 ). The repeatability of the sensing system is investigated by operating eight repeated measurements of 400nM melamine, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 0.54%, which suggested the reliability of the proposed sensor.

To demonstrate the selectivity of the proposed method, we investigate the fluorescence response of C-dots-AuNPs system to melamine at a concentration of 400nM in the presence of different interfering substances. As shown in Fig. 8 , compared with the intense fluorescence for melamine solution, no apparent enhancement of fluorescence intensity is detected for K+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Na+, SO4 2−, Cl−, NO3 −. The common species in raw milk, such as Ca2+, vitamin C, glucose, and lactose, still show no interferences. Since amino group (NH2–) and sulfydryl group (SH–) can interact with AuNPs, we further test some amino acids, such as glycine, histidine and cysteine. As shown in Fig. S6, no obvious fluorescence enhancement is observed with the concentration of 8μM for glycine and histidine except for cysteine. The reason for the interference of cysteine is supposed that the Au–S bond is much stronger than Au–N bond. Since the proteins are removed through the pretreatment of milk samples, we estimate that the interferences of cysteine can be avoided in the fluorescence assay for melamine used in milk samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of melamine in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality of the present method, the turn-on fluorescence assay is applied to detect melamine level in raw milk and milk powder. Certain amounts of melamine standard solution are directly spiked into the real samples. The analytical results for the pretreated milk samples spiked with certain amounts standard melamine are given in Table 2 . The good recoveries ranging from 90.475% to 111.35% with a RSD of around 2.05% definitely demonstrate the accuracy and reliability of the present fluorescence method for detecting melamine in practical applications.

In summary, a new fluorescence resonance transfer system between carbon dots and AuNPs was developed for the determination of melamine. With the addition of melamine, the FERT between C-dots and AuNPs was prevented and thus the fluorescence intensity of C-dots gradually increased. We optimized some important factors which would influence the FRET system. Under the optimum conditions, melamine could be detected with high sensitivity and low detection limit down to 36nM. Well performance in the determination of real samples was also obtained. Such a FRET system between C-dots and AuNPs may pave a new way for designing optical sensors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21275135), and the National Basic Research Program of China (973 Program, no. 2010CB933600).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2014.05.058.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

Energy transfer-based multiplexed assay of proteases by using gold nanoparticle and quantum dot conjugates on a surface
Analytical Chemistry
Visual detection of melamine in raw milk using gold nanoparticles as colorimetric probe
Food Chemistry

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Haichao Dai obtained her B.Sc. in 2010 from Southeast University, China. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Zhuang Li in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemsitry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Yan Shi obtained her B.Sc. in 2008 from Northeast University, China. Currently, she is is a graduate student under the supervision of Prof. Zhuang Li in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemsitry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Yilin Wang obtained her B.Sc. in 2012 from Zhengzhou University, China. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Zhuang Li in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemsitry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Yujing Sun recived her Ph.D. degree in 2010 from the University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, and her supervisor is Prof. Zhuang Li. At present, she works as a Research Associate in Zhuang Li's group at the Changchun Institute of Applied Chemsitry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Jingting Hu obtained her B.Sc. in 2008 from Dalian Nationalities University, China. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Zhuang Li in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemsitry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Pengjuan Ni obtained her B.Sc. in 2011 from Hebei University, China. Currently, she is pursuing her Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Zhuang Li in Changchun Institute of Applied Chemsitry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences.

Zhuang Li is a Professor of chemistry at Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences. Professor and Deputy Head of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences since 1998. His research interests include scanning probe microscopy; visioning DNA/protein and their interaction; nanomanipulations and nanodevices; ultrafast and accurate detection of trace chemical substances.
